refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), encompasses both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across multiple network scenarios and load conditions. Establish continuous monitoring of latency metrics in operational deployment.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, video, diagnostics) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency.
FR-TEL-002: Transmit telemetry data to remote operator with bounded network transit latency.
FR-TEL-003: Process operator commands and transmit control signals to vehicle actuators with bounded latency.
FR-TEL-004: Monitor and report latency metrics to enable operator awareness and system diagnostics.
FR-TEL-005: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency edge processing with local sensor buffering, prioritized network transmission (QoS), and optimized command-to-actuator pathways; monitor latency in real-time and enforce safety cutoffs.
DP-B: Deploy dedicated high-bandwidth, low-latency network infrastructure (private 5G or dedicated fiber) with redundant communication paths and hardware-accelerated packet processing.
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing optimization (DP-A) with network infrastructure investment (DP-B) and operator training on latency-aware control techniques.
DP-D: Implement predictive control and local autonomy fallback to reduce operator reaction time dependency; use network for supervisory commands only.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing optimization (sensor buffering, QoS-prioritized transmission, optimized command pathways), network infrastructure investment (dedicated or prioritized network with redundancy), real-time latency monitoring, and safety cutoff enforcement. This balances technical feasibility, cost, and safety margin.

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition latency (FR-TEL-001) and network transmission (FR-TEL-002): both must be optimized; decoupled via edge buffering and prioritization.
Coupling between command transmission (FR-TEL-003) and actuator response: decoupled via standardized control interfaces and deterministic actuator drivers.
Coupling between latency monitoring (FR-TEL-004) and safety enforcement (FR-TEL-005): tightly coupled by design; monitoring feeds safety logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits acceptable coupling. The hybrid approach allows independent optimization of sensor acquisition, network transit, and command execution. Latency monitoring and safety cutoff are intentionally coupled to ensure safety. No circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure can be provisioned or prioritized to support p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed conditions.
Assumption: Edge processing hardware (vehicle-mounted compute) has sufficient capacity for real-time sensor buffering and QoS management.
Risk: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed profile, latency may exceed threshold; mitigation via safety cutoff and operator notification.
Risk: Edge processing adds complexity and potential single points of failure; mitigation via redundancy and fallback modes.
Risk: Operator training and acceptance of latency-aware control techniques may be insufficient; mitigation via simulation and gradual deployment.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network infrastructure assumptions and acceptance criteria for 'agreed network conditions.' Define specific bandwidth, jitter, and packet loss profiles. Establish baseline latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transit, and command execution. Specify safety cutoff thresholds and operator notification mechanisms.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific functionality: sensor data acquisition and buffering, telemetry transmission, operator command reception and processing, control signal transmission to vehicle actuators, latency monitoring, and safety cutoff enforcement. Interfaces with Vehicle for sensor/actuator access and with Safety Services for safety cutoff coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits sensor data (position, video, diagnostics, latency metrics) to remote operator via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands and control signals via Tasking interface from remote operator.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module reports latency threshold violations and receives safety cutoff directives via SafetyAlert interface with Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link interface for bidirectional communication with remote operator infrastructure.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. High diffusion risk if teleoperation logic is scattered across multiple modules. Recommend Teleoperation as dedicated module within NGHE Solution.

Vehicle module focuses on physical platform, propulsion, and local control. Teleoperation is a remote operation capability that should not be tightly coupled to vehicle hardware. Placing teleoperation in Vehicle would diffuse vehicle responsibilities and create tight coupling to communication infrastructure. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation. All teleoperation-specific logic (sensor buffering, QoS transmission, command processing, latency monitoring, safety cutoff) naturally belongs here. Minimal diffusion risk. Interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (sensor/actuator access) and Safety Services (safety coordination). Strongly recommended.

Safety Services handles system-wide safety logic. Latency-based safety cutoff is a safety function but is tightly coupled to teleoperation operation. Recommend Safety Services coordinate cutoff policy, but Teleoperation module implements enforcement. Shared responsibility model.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation module (primary owner). Safety Services provides safety policy and cutoff coordination (secondary owner).
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains all teleoperation-specific functionality (sensor buffering, QoS transmission, command processing, latency monitoring). Safety Services owns safety cutoff policy and coordinates with Teleoperation via SafetyAlert interface. Vehicle module provides sensor/actuator interfaces without knowledge of teleoperation details.
containment_risks: Risk: Latency monitoring logic may be duplicated across Teleoperation and Safety Services if responsibilities are not clearly delineated. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns latency measurement; Safety Services owns cutoff policy.

Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient abstraction, exposing Teleoperation to network-specific details. Mitigation: Define Network Link as a clean abstraction layer; Teleoperation should not depend on specific network technology.

Risk: Vehicle module may not provide sensor/actuator interfaces with sufficient determinism for latency-critical teleoperation. Mitigation: Establish interface contracts with latency bounds; validate during integration.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Formalize interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle (sensor/actuator latency bounds), between Teleoperation and Safety Services (latency threshold reporting and cutoff coordination), and between Teleoperation and Network Link (QoS requirements and latency metrics). Define clear ownership of latency monitoring vs. safety cutoff policy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP-C (hybrid approach) directly addresses the refined FR. The DP specifies how to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms latency through edge processing optimization, network infrastructure investment, monitoring, and safety enforcement. The DP is feasible, testable, and aligns with high safety criticality. No circular dependencies or unresolvable conflicts detected.

diffusion_risks: Latency monitoring logic could diffuse across Teleoperation and Safety Services if interface contracts are not clear. Mitigation: Establish explicit SafetyAlert interface for latency reporting.
Edge processing optimization could diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation if sensor buffering responsibilities are not delineated. Mitigation: Define sensor interface contracts with latency bounds.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides strong containment for teleoperation-specific logic. Interfaces with Vehicle (sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (safety coordination), and Network Link (communication) are well-defined. Ownership boundaries are clear. Minimal diffusion risk with proper interface discipline.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency can be measured end-to-end via instrumentation in Teleoperation module and Network Link interface. Acceptance testing can validate p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions. Continuous monitoring in operational deployment provides ongoing verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005 are clearly allocated to Teleoperation module with supporting roles from Vehicle (sensor/actuator provision) and Safety Services (safety policy). Verification methods are defined for each sub-FR.

recommended_revisions: Formalize interface contracts (latency bounds, data formats, QoS requirements) between Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link.
Define network infrastructure assumptions and acceptance criteria for 'agreed network conditions' (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Establish latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transit, and command execution.
Specify safety cutoff thresholds, operator notification mechanisms, and fallback modes.
Plan operator training and acceptance testing for latency-aware control techniques.

decision.justification: The refined FR is decomposable into five sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005) that address distinct aspects of teleoperation latency: sensor acquisition, network transmission, command processing, monitoring, and safety enforcement. Each sub-FR requires further design detail and IM assignment. The selected DP-C is sound but requires decomposition into design parameters for each sub-FR. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve sufficient design detail for implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, video, diagnostics) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency.
FR-TEL-002: Transmit telemetry data to remote operator with bounded network transit latency.
FR-TEL-003: Process operator commands and transmit control signals to vehicle actuators with bounded latency.
FR-TEL-004: Monitor and report latency metrics to enable operator awareness and system diagnostics.
FR-TEL-005: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Network infrastructure can be provisioned or prioritized to support p95 ≤ 150 ms latency under agreed conditions.
Edge processing hardware (vehicle-mounted compute) has sufficient capacity for real-time sensor buffering and QoS management.
Vehicle module provides sensor/actuator interfaces with deterministic latency bounds.
Safety Services provides safety policy and cutoff coordination via SafetyAlert interface.
Operator training and acceptance of latency-aware control techniques will be conducted.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed profile, causing latency to exceed threshold.
Edge processing adds complexity and potential single points of failure.
Operator training and acceptance may be insufficient.
Interface contracts between Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link may not be sufficiently detailed.
Latency monitoring logic may diffuse across modules if ownership is not clear.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment and provisioning plan.
Edge processing hardware specifications and capacity analysis.
Sensor/actuator interface latency measurements from Vehicle module.
Safety cutoff policy and threshold definitions from Safety Services.
Operator acceptance testing results for latency-aware control.
Continuous latency monitoring data from operational deployment.
